fr:title.text: Further closure properties of truncation levels

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Further closure properties of truncation levels

fr:mainmatter.html:p: It is a proposition that a given type A is contractible.
The first and second steps follow from general computation lemmas for transport, and the last step follows from the fact that A is a set, which we may deduce from Lemma and Lemma .

fr:title.text: Being a proposition is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being a proposition is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: It is a proposition that a given type A is a proposition.
It is a proposition that a given type A is n-truncated.
We proceed by induction on the truncation level n.

html:ol.html:li: When n=-2, we recall from Lemma that being contractible is a proposition.
When n=-1, we recall from Lemma that being a proposition is a proposition.

fr:title.text: Being an n-truncated map is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being an n-truncated map is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: That a given map f\colon A \to B is n-truncated is a proposition.

fr:title.text: Being a surjection is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being a surjection is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: That a given map f\colon E\to B is surjective is a proposition.
By Corollary .

fr:title.text: n-truncated types are closed under retracts

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: n-truncated types are closed under retracts

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Any retract of an n-truncated type is n-truncated.

By induction on n; the base cases for n=-2 and n=-1 are given by Corollary and Lemma respectively. In the inductive step, we assume that s\colon B\to A exhibits B as a retract of A and that for each x,y:A the identification type x=_Ay is n+1-truncated to show that for all u,v:B the identification type u=_Bv is likewise n+1-truncated. The result follows from our assumption and the fact that u=_Bv is (by Lemma ) a retract of su=_Asv.
When s\colon A\to B is a section of r\colon B\to A, we say that A is a retract of B.
This follows immediately from univalence for functions.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.
Given a truncation level n, we define what it means for a type to be n-truncated by induction:

html:ul.html:li: a type is -2-truncated when it is contractible;
a type is -1-truncated when it is a proposition;
for n \geq 0, a type A is n-truncated when for every x,y:A the identification type x=y is n-1-truncated.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: So, in particular, a 0-truncated type is precisely a set. We shall often refer to n-truncated types as n-types.

fr:title.text: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The univalence principle for dependent functions
